concept.text: Our Earth has big pieces of land called continents and huge water called oceans.
concept.funFact: Most of Earth is covered in water!

questions.prompt: Is the ocean made of water?

concept.text: Mountains are very, very tall! Hills are smaller. Some mountains have snow on top.
concept.funFact: The tallest mountain is called Mount Everest!

concept.text: A river is water that flows. A lake is water that stays in one place.
concept.funFact: The Nile is one of the longest rivers in the world!

concept.text: Deserts are very hot and sandy. The North and South Poles are very cold and icy!
concept.funFact: Penguins live where it's very cold!

concept.text: Earth is the planet where we live. It is round like a ball and spins in space.
concept.funFact: Earth takes one whole day to spin around!

concept.text: Earth has seven continents: Africa, Antarctica, Asia, Australia, Europe, North America, and South America. Each has unique cultures, animals, and landscapes.
concept.funFact: Asia is the largest continent, while Australia is the smallest!

questions.prompt: The continent at the South Pole is ____.

concept.text: There are five oceans: Pacific, Atlantic, Indian, Southern, and Arctic. The Pacific is the largest and deepest of all.
concept.funFact: The Pacific Ocean is so big that all the continents could fit inside it!

questions.prompt: The ocean at the North Pole is the ____ Ocean.
Between Africa and Australia is the ____ Ocean.

concept.text: The Eiffel Tower stands in Paris, France. The Great Wall winds across China. The Statue of Liberty greets visitors in New York. Landmarks tell us about places and history.
concept.funFact: The Great Wall of China is over 13,000 miles long!

questions.prompt: The Statue of Liberty is in ____ City.

concept.text: Every country has a capital city — where the government works. Some famous capitals: Washington D.C. (USA), London (UK), Tokyo (Japan), and Canberra (Australia).
concept.funFact: Many people think Sydney is Australia's capital — but it's actually Canberra!

concept.text: Weather is what happens day to day — sunny, rainy, snowy. Climate is the pattern over many years. Tropical places stay warm; polar places stay cold.
concept.funFact: The Sahara Desert can reach over 120°F (50°C) in summer!

questions.prompt: Areas near the equator have a ____ climate.

concept.text: Earth's crust is broken into giant plates that slowly move. When they collide, mountains form. When they slip past each other, earthquakes shake. Volcanoes erupt where plates pull apart or dive under each other.
concept.funFact: The Pacific 'Ring of Fire' has about 75% of the world's active volcanoes.

questions.prompt: Mountains can form where two plates collide.
The Pacific ____ of Fire has many volcanoes.

concept.text: Capitals you should know: Brazil → Brasília, Canada → Ottawa, India → New Delhi, South Africa has three (Pretoria, Cape Town, Bloemfontein), and Russia → Moscow.
concept.funFact: Bolivia also has two capitals — Sucre (constitutional) and La Paz (seat of government).

questions.prompt: South Africa has three capital cities.

concept.text: Earth has several climate zones: tropical (near the equator), arid (deserts), temperate (mild seasons), polar (frozen), and Mediterranean (warm dry summers, mild wet winters).
concept.funFact: The Atacama Desert in Chile is the driest place on Earth — some areas have never recorded rain!

questions.prompt: Which climate is near the equator?
The driest desert in the world is the ____ Desert.
The Mediterranean climate has hot, wet summers.
Order by average rainfall, lowest to highest.

concept.text: The Nile (Africa) and Amazon (South America) are the two longest rivers on Earth. The Mississippi drains much of the USA. The Yangtze is Asia's longest river.
concept.funFact: The Amazon River discharges more water than the next seven largest rivers combined!

questions.prompt: The Amazon River is in Africa.
The longest river in Africa is the ____.
The Mississippi River is mostly in...

concept.text: Latitude lines run east-west and measure how far north or south of the equator. Longitude lines run north-south. Together they make a coordinate grid for any place on Earth.
concept.funFact: Greenwich, England is at 0° longitude — the Prime Meridian!

questions.prompt: Lines that run east-west are called...
The Prime Meridian runs through ____, England.
